findings.description: Page 1 layout is looser and the header region is reorganized compared with the PDF.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, the memorandum heading, date, addressee/from blocks, and subject are positioned with wider left margin spacing and larger whitespace gaps; in the HTML, the same content is preserved but compressed vertically with a horizontal rule and tighter block spacing.

findings.description: Text styling on page 1 differs slightly, including heading emphasis and inserted signature metadata styling.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows 'MEMORANDUM' in an underlined serif style and body text in serif fonts; the HTML uses a bold sans-serif heading with a rule underneath and renders the digital signature lines in italicized text not visually matching the source.

findings.description: Page 2 layout differs due to merged running header/footer elements and altered whitespace.
findings.evidence: In the PDF, page 2 has a small running header at top right and a clean page body starting with 'I. History/Background'; in the HTML, the page 1 footer bar and page 2 running header are visually merged into the flow before the section heading, changing spacing but not content.

findings.description: Section heading formatting on page 2 is not fully preserved.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses a bold serif heading with roman numeral formatting; the HTML renders 'I. History/Background' in a larger sans-serif style, with less visual similarity to the source though the hierarchy remains clear.

findings.description: Page 3 layout is preserved overall, but running header placement and spacing differ.
findings.evidence: The PDF places the meeting report header compactly at the top right with body text beginning below; the HTML carries over prior-page flow and places the running header lower relative to surrounding content, altering page grouping without changing the sequence.

findings.description: Bullet list typography and heading styling on page 3 are visually simplified.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses serif text with bold-underlined topic labels in bullets; the HTML keeps bullets and emphasis but in sans-serif with lighter visual distinction and slightly different line wrapping.

findings.description: Page 4 content grouping is preserved but page-level structure is flattened in HTML.
findings.evidence: The PDF separates the last bullet from section II and starts section III with a distinct page header; the HTML presents this as a continuous scroll layout with the running header embedded mid-flow, reducing the original page-based visual grouping.

findings.description: Section III heading and bullet emphasis on page 4 are formatted differently from the PDF.
findings.evidence: The PDF uses bold serif section numbering and bold-underlined bullet labels; the HTML uses sans-serif headings and bold labels without matching the original typographic style exactly.

findings.description: Page 5 layout is functionally preserved but the attachment list is more compact and page header placement differs.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows the top-right running header and a clearly separated attachments block with generous whitespace; the HTML presents the same content in a continuous scroll with tighter spacing and the header embedded above the final bullets.

findings.description: Attachment list formatting on page 5 differs slightly from the source.
findings.evidence: The PDF shows roman-numbered attachments in serif text with wider indentation; the HTML preserves the list items and numbering but uses sans-serif styling and more compact indentation.

findings.description: HTML includes fabricated digital-signature text and an email address not present in the source PDF.
findings.evidence: HTML adds: "Digitally signed by Elizabeth S Werner ... email=elizabeth.werner@ncdenr.gov ... Date: 2016.04.28 09:56:21 -04'00'". On the PDF first page, under "From: Elizabeth S. Werner / Division of Waste Management / Raleigh Central Office," no such digital-signature block, DN metadata, or email address appears.

findings.description: There are text inaccuracies and OCR/conversion errors that alter names and wording.
findings.evidence: HTML uses "o=DWM-Soild Waste" in the fabricated signature block, which is also misspelled. In the oral comments section, HTML says "there wells" instead of the PDF's "their wells" in the sentence about testing wells outside the 1500-ft boundary. These are not just formatting changes.
